title: Maritime OSINT: Ukraine Targets Russian Shadow Fleet Tankers as Sanctions Evasion Network Grows
summary: Maritime intelligence analysts track the growing Russian shadow oil fleet and Ukrainian strikes targeting vessels near Novorossiysk, highlighting the intersection of maritime sanctions enforcement and military operations.
contents:
## Russian Shadow Fleet Under Attack — Maritime Intelligence Update

### Ukrainian Strike Operations
Ukraine's military struck two Russian shadow fleet tankers near the entrance to Novorossiysk port, with President Zelenskyy describing the operation as "applying sanctions against Russia's shadow oil fleet." The vessels had actively participated in transporting Russian crude oil outside the international sanctions framework.

### Shadow Fleet Scale
Maritime OSINT tracking identifies a growing fleet of aging tankers operating outside international insurance and regulatory frameworks to transport Russian oil. These vessels typically fly flags of convenience from nations such as Gabon, Cameroon, Palau, and St. Kitts, and receive management through front companies in various jurisdictions. The average age of shadow fleet vessels exceeds 17 years, raising significant environmental and safety concerns.

### Sanctions Evasion Infrastructure
The shadow fleet represents a sophisticated sanctions evasion network that enables Russia to maintain oil export volumes despite Western restrictions. Ship-to-ship transfers in international waters, frequent renaming and reflagging, and complex corporate ownership structures through shell companies complicate enforcement efforts.

### Global Energy Market Impact
The International Energy Agency reported that Russian crude and oil product exports reached 7.1 million barrels per day in March 2026, with revenues nearly doubling to $19 billion due to Iran war-driven price increases. The Hormuz blockade has paradoxically benefited Russian oil revenues even as Ukrainian strikes target the physical infrastructure enabling exports.

### Port Security Assessment
Novorossiysk remains Russia's primary oil export terminal on the Black Sea. The successful targeting of vessels at the port entrance demonstrates Ukraine's growing ability to threaten maritime logistics deep within Russian territory, potentially forcing Russia to shift export operations or invest in additional port defense systems.
